chapter: ENG9_4 – In the Kingdom of Fools
title: In the Kingdom of Fools – Medium LAQs

questions.question: Explain the strange laws introduced by the king in the Kingdom of Fools.
questions.answer: The king ordered that night should be treated as day and day as night. People had to stay awake and work during the night and sleep during the day. Anyone who disobeyed this order would be punished with death. This rule created an unnatural lifestyle for the people.

questions.question: Describe the reaction of the guru and the disciple when they first entered the kingdom.
questions.answer: When the guru and disciple entered the kingdom, they found the town completely silent during the day. They later realised that the people were sleeping because of the king’s strange law. They were amazed and confused by the unusual system of the kingdom.

questions.question: Why did the guru warn the disciple not to stay in the kingdom?
questions.answer: The guru realised that the kingdom was ruled by foolish leaders who made irrational decisions. He believed that living in such a place was dangerous because unpredictable actions could harm innocent people.

questions.question: Explain why the disciple decided to stay in the Kingdom of Fools.
questions.answer: The disciple stayed because he discovered that everything in the market cost the same price. He could buy large amounts of food very cheaply. His greed and love for food made him ignore the guru’s warning.

questions.question: Describe the market system in the Kingdom of Fools.
questions.answer: In the market, all goods were sold at the same price, one duddu. Whether someone bought rice, bananas, or any other item, the cost remained the same. This strange economic system showed the foolishness of the kingdom’s rulers.

questions.question: Explain the incident that caused the thief’s death.
questions.answer: A thief broke into a merchant’s house through a hole in the wall. While he was leaving with stolen goods, the weak wall collapsed and fell on him, killing him instantly.

questions.question: Why did the thief’s brother complain to the king?
questions.answer: The thief’s brother complained because he believed the merchant was responsible for the weak wall that caused his brother’s death. He demanded justice and punishment for the merchant.

questions.question: Describe the chain of blame in the king’s court.
questions.answer: The blame for the thief’s death passed from the merchant to the bricklayer, then to the dancing girl, and finally to the goldsmith. Each person tried to avoid punishment by blaming someone else.

questions.question: Why did the bricklayer blame the dancing girl?
questions.answer: The bricklayer said he was distracted by the sound of the dancing girl’s anklets while building the wall. Because of this distraction, he could not concentrate on his work.

questions.question: Why did the goldsmith blame the merchant?
questions.answer: The goldsmith said he delayed the dancer’s jewellery because he had to complete the merchant’s order first.

questions.question: Why did the king finally order the merchant’s execution?
questions.answer: The king concluded that the merchant’s family was responsible for the wall that caused the thief’s death. Therefore he decided that the merchant must be punished.

questions.question: Why was the merchant not executed in the end?
questions.answer: The merchant was too thin to fit the execution stake. Therefore the king ordered his servants to find someone fat enough.

questions.question: How was the disciple chosen for execution?
questions.answer: The disciple had grown fat after eating cheap food every day. When the king’s men searched for someone fat enough, they found him and chose him.

questions.question: Describe the disciple’s reaction when he was taken for execution.
questions.answer: The disciple became frightened and remembered the guru’s warning. He realised that his greed had brought him into danger.

questions.question: How did the guru return to save the disciple?
questions.answer: The guru had magical powers and saw the disciple’s danger in a vision. Therefore he immediately returned to the kingdom.

questions.question: Explain the trick used by the guru to save the disciple.
questions.answer: The guru told the king that whoever died first on the stake would be reborn as king of the kingdom. This made the king greedy and eager to die first.

questions.question: Why did the king postpone the execution?
questions.answer: The king wanted time to think about the guru’s mysterious words and to discuss the matter with his minister.

questions.question: What plan did the king and minister make secretly?
questions.answer: They decided to replace the guru and disciple secretly and die on the stake themselves so they could become rulers again in the next life.

questions.question: What happened during the execution at night?
questions.answer: The king and minister secretly replaced the guru and disciple and were executed on the stake by the servants.

questions.question: How did the people react after the king’s death?
questions.answer: The people panicked and were confused because the kingdom suddenly had no ruler.

questions.question: Why did the people request the guru to rule the kingdom?
questions.answer: They believed the guru was wise and capable of ruling the kingdom properly.

questions.question: What condition did the guru set before accepting the throne?
questions.answer: He said he would rule only if he was allowed to change all the foolish laws of the kingdom.

questions.question: What reforms were introduced after the guru became king?
questions.answer: He restored the natural order where people worked during the day and slept at night. The strange pricing system was also ended.

questions.question: Explain the humour present in the story.
questions.answer: The story uses humorous situations such as the chain of blame and the foolish decisions of the king. These events make the story entertaining while delivering a message.

questions.question: What message does the story convey about leadership?
questions.answer: The story shows that foolish leaders create confusion and injustice, while wise leadership brings order and stability.
